Week 3 Study Guide: Missing Data & Power Analysis

Statistical Modelling

1 Key Topics

  • Identifying and handling missing data in R
  • Visualising missing data patterns using the VIM and summarytools packages
  • Strategies for dealing with missing data: removal vs. imputation
  • Iterative imputation with MICE and irmi()
  • What statistical power means and why it matters
  • The four interconnected quantities in power analysis: sample size, effect size, α, and power
  • Calculating power manually for a Z-test
  • Using power analysis to determine required sample size
  • Linking power analysis to confidence intervals and margins of error

2 Part 1: Missing Data

2.1 Core Explanation

2.1.1 What Is Missing Data?

In real-world datasets, it is very common to find that some values are simply not recorded — these are called missing values, represented in R as NA (Not Available). Missing data can arise for many reasons: a participant dropped out of a study, a sensor failed to record, or a question was left blank on a survey.

Before doing any analysis, it is important to understand how much data is missing and where it is missing from, because this can significantly affect your results.

2.1.2 The Sleep Dataset

The examples in this week’s class used the sleep dataset — a study of sleep patterns across 39 mammal species (62 observations, 10 variables). This dataset was chosen because it has a realistic spread of missing values across several columns.

The variables in the dataset are:

Variable Description
BodyWgt Body weight (kg)
BrainWgt Brain weight (kg)
NonD Non-dream sleep (hours/day)
Dream Dream sleep (hours/day)
Sleep Total sleep (NonD + Dream)
Span Max life span (years)
Gest Gestation time (days)
Pred Predation index (danger from predators)
Exp Sleep exposure index
Danger Overall danger index (1 = least, 5 = most)

2.1.3 Basic R Functions for Missing Data

When you only have a few missing values and no obvious patterns, R provides several straightforward tools:

is.na(x)               # Returns TRUE/FALSE for each value — is it missing?
which(is.na(x))        # Tells you the *position* (index) of the NAs
na.rm = TRUE           # Used inside functions like mean() to ignore NAs
complete.cases(x)      # Returns TRUE for rows with no missing values
na.omit(x)             # Removes all rows that contain any NA

Removing rows based on conditions:

# Remove rows where a specific column has NA
df <- df[!is.na(df$specific_column), ]

# Remove rows where EITHER col1 OR col2 has NA
df <- df[!(is.na(df$col1) | is.na(df$col2)), ]

2.1.4 Summarising Your Data with summarytools

The summarytools package provides a quick way to see the distribution of each variable and spot where values are missing. It produces a Data Frame Summary table showing the mean, standard deviation, min/max, and the number of distinct valid values for each column.

library(summarytools)
dfSummary(sleep)

This is a good first step — it gives you a bird’s-eye view of the entire dataset, including which variables have fewer valid values (a sign of missingness).

2.2 Visualising Missing Data

The VIM package provides powerful tools specifically for exploring missing data visually. The key principle is that red = missing and blue = complete across all VIM plots.

2.2.1 aggr() — Count and Plot Missingness

library(VIM)
aggr(sleep, numbers = TRUE, prop = FALSE)

This produces two plots: 1. A bar chart showing how many values are missing per variable (e.g., NonD had ~14 missing values in the sleep dataset). 2. A combination plot showing which combinations of variables tend to be missing together. For example, rows 31 and 62 both had NonD, Dream, and Sleep missing simultaneously — which makes sense because Sleep = NonD + Dream.

2.2.2 barMiss() — Missingness by a Categorical Variable

barMiss(sleep[, c("Exp", "Sleep")])

Use this when you want to see whether missingness in one variable is related to the values of another variable. For the sleep data, Sleep had 4 missing values: 1 occurred when Exp = 1, and 3 occurred when Exp = 5. Exp itself had no missing values.

2.2.3 histMiss() — Missingness by a Continuous Variable

histMiss(sleep[, c("Span", "Sleep")])

This is the same idea as barMiss() but for continuous variables, where a histogram is more appropriate. For the sleep data, the 4 missing Sleep values appeared in cases where Span was between 10–20 years and 20–30 years.

2.2.4 marginplot() — Comparing Distributions With and Without Missingness

marginplot(sleep[, c("Span", "Sleep")])

This is one of the most informative plots. It shows a scatter plot of two variables, with extra boxplots in the margins:

  • The red boxplot shows the distribution of Sleep for cases where Span is missing — i.e., what does Sleep look like when we don’t know Span?
  • The blue boxplot shows the distribution of Sleep for cases where Span is observed.

If the red and blue boxplots look very similar, it suggests the data may be Missing At Random (MAR) — the missingness is not related to the unobserved values. If they look very different, the missing data pattern may be informative.

Annotation space: How did the red and blue boxplots compare in the sleep example? What might that suggest about the missing data mechanism?

2.3 Dealing with Missing Data

2.3.1 Option 1: Complete Case Analysis (Listwise Deletion)

The simplest approach is to just remove any row that contains a missing value. In R’s lm() function, this is actually the default behaviour — it automatically uses only complete cases.

# These two are equivalent for linear regression:
lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt, data = sleep)
lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt, data = na.omit(sleep))

Other related options:

na.fail()     # Throws an error if any NAs are present — forces you to address it
na.exclude()  # Like na.omit() but keeps NA slots in predictions

When is this acceptable? Only when the amount of missing data is small and there is no systematic pattern to which cases are missing. If certain types of cases are more likely to be missing, removing them can bias your results.

2.3.2 Option 2: Imputation with irmi() (VIM Package)

Imputation means filling in the missing values with estimated values, rather than throwing the data away.

The irmi() function uses Iterative Robust Model-based Imputation:

library(VIM)
imputed_sleep <- irmi(sleep)

How it works: 1. Take one variable (e.g., NonD) as the response variable. 2. Use all other variables as predictors in a regression model. 3. Use the predicted values from that model to fill in the missing NonD values. 4. Repeat this process, cycling through each variable that has missing values. 5. Continue iterating until the imputed values stabilise.

After imputation, you should compare linear model results before and after imputation to see how much the coefficients changed. In the sleep example, the intercept was slightly larger and the slope slightly less negative after imputation.

2.3.3 Option 3: Multiple Imputation with MICE

The MICE package (Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations) is a more sophisticated approach. Instead of imputing once, it creates multiple complete datasets (e.g., 5), fits a model to each one, and then pools the results.

library(mice)
imputed <- mice(sleep, m = 5, method = "pmm")  # pmm = predictive mean matching
models  <- with(imputed, lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt))
pooled  <- pool(models)
summary(pooled)

Predictive Mean Matching (PMM) works by finding observed values that are close to the predicted value and using one of those as the imputed value. This keeps imputed values realistic (they come from real observed data).

The predictor matrix in MICE output tells you which variables were used to predict the missing values in each column.

Why does the pooled model have larger standard errors? Because pooling across 5 datasets accounts for the extra uncertainty introduced by the fact that we don’t truly know what the missing values are. This is actually more honest than a single imputation.

Annotation space: When would you choose MICE over irmi()? Think about dataset size, the proportion of missing data, and how much you care about uncertainty quantification.

3 Part 2: Power Analysis

3.1 Core Explanation

3.1.1 What Is Statistical Power?

When you run a hypothesis test, there are two ways you can be wrong:

Decision H₀ is True H₀ is False
Reject H₀ Type I error (probability = α) ✅ Correct! (probability = power = 1 − β)
Fail to reject H₀ ✅ Correct! (probability = 1 − α) Type II error (probability = β)

Statistical power is defined as:

\[\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})\]

In plain English: power is the probability that your test will correctly detect a real effect when one actually exists. A higher power means a better, more sensitive test.

3.1.2 The Four Interconnected Quantities

Power analysis is built on the relationship between four quantities. If you know any three, you can calculate the fourth:

  1. Sample size \(n\) — larger samples give more power
  2. Effect size — the size of the difference you’re trying to detect (larger effects are easier to detect)
  3. Significance level \(\alpha\) — the threshold for rejecting \(H_0\) (typically 0.05)
  4. Power \(1 - \beta\) — typically you aim for at least 0.80 (80%)

Important caveat: In practice, you cannot know the true effect size — if you already knew the true difference, there would be no need to test! Instead, you define the minimum effect size of practical importance (the smallest difference that would actually matter for your application).

3.2 Key Equations / Formulas

3.2.1 Z-Test Statistic (known population standard deviation)

\[Z_{stat} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\]

3.2.2 Minimum Sample Mean to Reject \(H_0\)

\[\bar{X}_{crit} = \mu + \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \times Z_{crit}\]

3.2.3 Power Calculation

Once you have \(\bar{X}_{crit}\), calculate the probability of exceeding it under the alternative hypothesis:

\[\beta = P\left(\bar{X} < \bar{X}_{crit} \mid \mu = \mu_A\right) = P\left(Z < \frac{\bar{X}_{crit} - \mu_A}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\right)\]

\[\text{Power} = 1 - \beta\]

3.2.4 Sample Size Formula (known σ, from margin of error)

\[n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \times \sigma}{ME}\right)^2\]

3.2.5 Sample Size Formula (unknown σ, using t-distribution)

\[n = \left(\frac{t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \times s}{ME}\right)^2\]

(Note: this requires iteration since \(t\) depends on \(n\))

3.3 Practical Example — Chemical Company

3.3.1 Problem Setup

A chemical company claims their product contains on average μ = 4.0 ml of caustic material per litre. A government inspector tests a sample of n = 30 containers and finds: - Sample mean: \(\bar{x} = 4.3\) ml - Known population standard deviation: \(\sigma = 1.3\)

3.3.2 Step 1: State the Hypotheses

Since the product is only dangerous if it contains more than 4.0 ml, this is a one-tailed test:

\[H_0: \mu = 4.0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_A: \mu > 4.0\]

3.3.3 Step 2: Type I and Type II Errors in Context

  • Type I error: Concluding the mean is greater than 4 ml when it actually isn’t — falsely accusing the company.
  • Type II error: Concluding the mean is not greater than 4 ml when it actually is — missing a genuine danger.

3.3.4 Step 3: Conduct the Test (α = 0.05)

\[Z_{stat} = \frac{4.3 - 4.0}{1.3 / \sqrt{30}} = \frac{0.3}{0.2374} = 1.264\]

  • Critical value for a one-tailed test at α = 0.05: \(Z_{crit} = 1.645\)
  • Since \(Z_{stat} = 1.264 < 1.645\), we fail to reject \(H_0\)
  • The p-value is 0.103 > 0.05, confirming this conclusion
  • 95% lower bound: 3.910 (we are 95% confident the true mean is above 3.910 ml)

Conclusion: The data does not provide sufficient evidence that the mean caustic level exceeds 4 ml/litre.

3.3.5 Step 4: Calculate the Power of the Test

Now ask: Was our sample large enough to detect a meaningful difference?

Suppose we define a difference of 0.35 ml/litre as practically significant — i.e., we want to detect if the true mean is μ_A = 4.35.

Find the critical sample mean (threshold for rejection):

\[\bar{X}_{crit} = 4.0 + \frac{1.3}{\sqrt{30}} \times 1.645 = 4.0 + 0.39 = 4.39\]

So we only reject \(H_0\) if the sample mean exceeds 4.39.

Calculate β (probability of Type II error):

\[\beta = P(\bar{X} < 4.39 \mid \mu = 4.35) = P\left(Z < \frac{4.39 - 4.35}{1.3/\sqrt{30}}\right) = P(Z < 0.17) = 0.5675\]

Power:

\[\text{Power} = 1 - 0.5675 = \mathbf{0.4325}\]

Interpretation: There is only a 43.25% chance of correctly detecting a 0.35 ml difference with n = 30. This is quite low — the test is underpowered. We would ideally want power ≥ 0.80.

Annotation space: What would you recommend the inspector do? Collect a larger sample? Define a larger minimum effect size? Both options involve trade-offs.

3.4 Sample Size from Margin of Error

Power analysis is closely linked to confidence intervals. The margin of error (ME) in a confidence interval is:

\[ME = Z_{\alpha/2} \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]

Rearranging to find the required sample size for a desired ME:

\[n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \times \sigma}{ME}\right)^2\]

Example: An analyst wants to estimate mean voltage (σ = 22.5 volts) with a margin of error of 5 volts at 95% confidence:

\[n = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 22.5}{5}\right)^2 = (8.82)^2 \approx 78 \text{ observations}\]

When \(\sigma\) is unknown, replace \(Z\) with the \(t\)-distribution critical value and \(\sigma\) with the sample standard deviation \(s\).

4 Summary Points

  • Missing data is common in real datasets — before removing or imputing, always visualise and understand the pattern.
  • The VIM package functions (aggr, barMiss, histMiss, marginplot) help you explore where and why data is missing.
  • Complete case analysis (na.omit) is simple but can bias results if the missing data is not random.
  • irmi() performs iterative regression-based imputation; MICE performs multiple imputation and is more robust for uncertainty quantification.
  • Statistical power (1 − β) is the probability of correctly detecting a real effect — aim for at least 0.80.
  • Power depends on four quantities: sample size, effect size, α, and power (1 − β). Know three, find the fourth.
  • Low power (like 43% in the example) means a test may fail to detect a real, meaningful difference — the sample was simply too small.
  • You can use the margin of error formula to calculate the minimum sample size needed for a given precision, whether σ is known (Z-formula) or unknown (t-formula).

5 Exercises to Try

  1. Recalculate the power of the caustic material test if the minimum detectable increase is 0.1 ml instead of 0.35 ml. (Answer: 0.1112)
  2. An analyst wants to estimate mean voltage (σ = 22.5 volts). What sample size achieves a margin of error of 5 volts at 95% confidence for a two-sided CI? (Answer: n = 78)
  3. In the sleep dataset, which variable had the most missing values? Use aggr() to verify.
  4. Why does the pooled MICE model have larger standard errors than the individual imputed models?
Week 2
Week 4
Source Code
---
title: "Week 3 Study Guide: Missing Data & Power Analysis"
subtitle: "Statistical Modelling"
---

# Key Topics

- Identifying and handling **missing data** in R
- Visualising missing data patterns using the **VIM** and **summarytools** packages
- Strategies for dealing with missing data: removal vs. imputation
- Iterative imputation with **MICE** and **irmi()**
- What **statistical power** means and why it matters
- The four interconnected quantities in power analysis: sample size, effect size, α, and power
- Calculating power manually for a Z-test
- Using power analysis to determine required **sample size**
- Linking power analysis to **confidence intervals** and **margins of error**

---

# Part 1: Missing Data

## Core Explanation

### What Is Missing Data?

In real-world datasets, it is very common to find that some values are simply not recorded — these are called **missing values**, represented in R as `NA` (Not Available). Missing data can arise for many reasons: a participant dropped out of a study, a sensor failed to record, or a question was left blank on a survey.

Before doing any analysis, it is important to understand *how much* data is missing and *where* it is missing from, because this can significantly affect your results.

### The Sleep Dataset

The examples in this week's class used the **sleep dataset** — a study of sleep patterns across 39 mammal species (62 observations, 10 variables). This dataset was chosen because it has a realistic spread of missing values across several columns.

The variables in the dataset are:

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `BodyWgt` | Body weight (kg) |
| `BrainWgt` | Brain weight (kg) |
| `NonD` | Non-dream sleep (hours/day) |
| `Dream` | Dream sleep (hours/day) |
| `Sleep` | Total sleep (NonD + Dream) |
| `Span` | Max life span (years) |
| `Gest` | Gestation time (days) |
| `Pred` | Predation index (danger from predators) |
| `Exp` | Sleep exposure index |
| `Danger` | Overall danger index (1 = least, 5 = most) |

### Basic R Functions for Missing Data

When you only have a few missing values and no obvious patterns, R provides several straightforward tools:

```r
is.na(x)               # Returns TRUE/FALSE for each value — is it missing?
which(is.na(x))        # Tells you the *position* (index) of the NAs
na.rm = TRUE           # Used inside functions like mean() to ignore NAs
complete.cases(x)      # Returns TRUE for rows with no missing values
na.omit(x)             # Removes all rows that contain any NA
```

**Removing rows based on conditions:**

```r
# Remove rows where a specific column has NA
df <- df[!is.na(df$specific_column), ]

# Remove rows where EITHER col1 OR col2 has NA
df <- df[!(is.na(df$col1) | is.na(df$col2)), ]
```

### Summarising Your Data with `summarytools`

The `summarytools` package provides a quick way to see the distribution of each variable and spot where values are missing. It produces a **Data Frame Summary** table showing the mean, standard deviation, min/max, and the number of distinct valid values for each column.

```r
library(summarytools)
dfSummary(sleep)
```

This is a good first step — it gives you a bird's-eye view of the entire dataset, including which variables have fewer valid values (a sign of missingness).

---

## Visualising Missing Data

The **VIM** package provides powerful tools specifically for exploring missing data visually. The key principle is that **red = missing** and **blue = complete** across all VIM plots.

### `aggr()` — Count and Plot Missingness

```r
library(VIM)
aggr(sleep, numbers = TRUE, prop = FALSE)
```

This produces two plots:
1. A **bar chart** showing how many values are missing per variable (e.g., `NonD` had ~14 missing values in the sleep dataset).
2. A **combination plot** showing which *combinations* of variables tend to be missing together. For example, rows 31 and 62 both had `NonD`, `Dream`, and `Sleep` missing simultaneously — which makes sense because `Sleep = NonD + Dream`.

### `barMiss()` — Missingness by a Categorical Variable

```r
barMiss(sleep[, c("Exp", "Sleep")])
```

Use this when you want to see whether missingness in one variable is related to the *values* of another variable. For the sleep data, `Sleep` had 4 missing values: 1 occurred when `Exp = 1`, and 3 occurred when `Exp = 5`. `Exp` itself had no missing values.

### `histMiss()` — Missingness by a Continuous Variable

```r
histMiss(sleep[, c("Span", "Sleep")])
```

This is the same idea as `barMiss()` but for continuous variables, where a **histogram** is more appropriate. For the sleep data, the 4 missing `Sleep` values appeared in cases where `Span` was between 10–20 years and 20–30 years.

### `marginplot()` — Comparing Distributions With and Without Missingness

```r
marginplot(sleep[, c("Span", "Sleep")])
```

This is one of the most informative plots. It shows a **scatter plot** of two variables, with extra boxplots in the margins:

- The **red boxplot** shows the distribution of `Sleep` *for cases where `Span` is missing* — i.e., what does `Sleep` look like when we don't know `Span`?
- The **blue boxplot** shows the distribution of `Sleep` *for cases where `Span` is observed*.

If the red and blue boxplots look very similar, it suggests the data may be **Missing At Random (MAR)** — the missingness is not related to the unobserved values. If they look very different, the missing data pattern may be informative.

> **Annotation space:** *How did the red and blue boxplots compare in the sleep example? What might that suggest about the missing data mechanism?*

---

## Dealing with Missing Data

### Option 1: Complete Case Analysis (Listwise Deletion)

The simplest approach is to just remove any row that contains a missing value. In R's `lm()` function, this is actually the **default behaviour** — it automatically uses only complete cases.

```r
# These two are equivalent for linear regression:
lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt, data = sleep)
lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt, data = na.omit(sleep))
```

Other related options:

```r
na.fail()     # Throws an error if any NAs are present — forces you to address it
na.exclude()  # Like na.omit() but keeps NA slots in predictions
```

**When is this acceptable?** Only when the amount of missing data is small and there is no systematic pattern to *which* cases are missing. If certain types of cases are more likely to be missing, removing them can **bias** your results.

### Option 2: Imputation with `irmi()` (VIM Package)

**Imputation** means filling in the missing values with estimated values, rather than throwing the data away.

The `irmi()` function uses **Iterative Robust Model-based Imputation**:

```r
library(VIM)
imputed_sleep <- irmi(sleep)
```

**How it works:**
1. Take one variable (e.g., `NonD`) as the *response* variable.
2. Use all other variables as *predictors* in a regression model.
3. Use the predicted values from that model to fill in the missing `NonD` values.
4. Repeat this process, cycling through each variable that has missing values.
5. Continue iterating until the imputed values stabilise.

After imputation, you should **compare** linear model results before and after imputation to see how much the coefficients changed. In the sleep example, the intercept was slightly larger and the slope slightly less negative after imputation.

### Option 3: Multiple Imputation with MICE

The **MICE** package (Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations) is a more sophisticated approach. Instead of imputing once, it creates **multiple** complete datasets (e.g., 5), fits a model to each one, and then **pools** the results.

```r
library(mice)
imputed <- mice(sleep, m = 5, method = "pmm")  # pmm = predictive mean matching
models  <- with(imputed, lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt))
pooled  <- pool(models)
summary(pooled)
```

**Predictive Mean Matching (PMM)** works by finding observed values that are close to the predicted value and using one of those as the imputed value. This keeps imputed values realistic (they come from real observed data).

The **predictor matrix** in MICE output tells you which variables were used to predict the missing values in each column.

**Why does the pooled model have larger standard errors?** Because pooling across 5 datasets accounts for the *extra uncertainty* introduced by the fact that we don't truly know what the missing values are. This is actually more honest than a single imputation.

> **Annotation space:** *When would you choose MICE over irmi()? Think about dataset size, the proportion of missing data, and how much you care about uncertainty quantification.*

---

# Part 2: Power Analysis

## Core Explanation

### What Is Statistical Power?

When you run a hypothesis test, there are two ways you can be wrong:

| Decision | H₀ is True | H₀ is False |
|----------|------------|-------------|
| **Reject H₀** | Type I error (probability = α) | ✅ Correct! (probability = power = 1 − β) |
| **Fail to reject H₀** | ✅ Correct! (probability = 1 − α) | Type II error (probability = β) |

**Statistical power** is defined as:

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})$$

In plain English: power is the probability that your test will correctly detect a real effect when one actually exists. A higher power means a better, more sensitive test.

### The Four Interconnected Quantities

Power analysis is built on the relationship between four quantities. **If you know any three, you can calculate the fourth:**

1. **Sample size** $n$ — larger samples give more power
2. **Effect size** — the size of the difference you're trying to detect (larger effects are easier to detect)
3. **Significance level** $\alpha$ — the threshold for rejecting $H_0$ (typically 0.05)
4. **Power** $1 - \beta$ — typically you aim for at least 0.80 (80%)

> **Important caveat:** In practice, you cannot know the *true* effect size — if you already knew the true difference, there would be no need to test! Instead, you define the **minimum effect size of practical importance** (the smallest difference that would actually matter for your application).

---

## Key Equations / Formulas

### Z-Test Statistic (known population standard deviation)

$$Z_{stat} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

### Minimum Sample Mean to Reject $H_0$

$$\bar{X}_{crit} = \mu + \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \times Z_{crit}$$

### Power Calculation

Once you have $\bar{X}_{crit}$, calculate the probability of exceeding it under the alternative hypothesis:

$$\beta = P\left(\bar{X} < \bar{X}_{crit} \mid \mu = \mu_A\right) = P\left(Z < \frac{\bar{X}_{crit} - \mu_A}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\right)$$

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta$$

### Sample Size Formula (known σ, from margin of error)

$$n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \times \sigma}{ME}\right)^2$$

### Sample Size Formula (unknown σ, using t-distribution)

$$n = \left(\frac{t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \times s}{ME}\right)^2$$

*(Note: this requires iteration since $t$ depends on $n$)*

---

## Practical Example — Chemical Company

### Problem Setup

A chemical company claims their product contains on average **μ = 4.0 ml** of caustic material per litre. A government inspector tests a sample of **n = 30** containers and finds:
- Sample mean: $\bar{x} = 4.3$ ml
- Known population standard deviation: $\sigma = 1.3$

### Step 1: State the Hypotheses

Since the product is only dangerous if it contains *more* than 4.0 ml, this is a **one-tailed test**:

$$H_0: \mu = 4.0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_A: \mu > 4.0$$

### Step 2: Type I and Type II Errors in Context

- **Type I error:** Concluding the mean *is* greater than 4 ml when it actually isn't — falsely accusing the company.
- **Type II error:** Concluding the mean is *not* greater than 4 ml when it actually is — missing a genuine danger.

### Step 3: Conduct the Test (α = 0.05)

$$Z_{stat} = \frac{4.3 - 4.0}{1.3 / \sqrt{30}} = \frac{0.3}{0.2374} = 1.264$$

- Critical value for a one-tailed test at α = 0.05: $Z_{crit} = 1.645$
- Since $Z_{stat} = 1.264 < 1.645$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**
- The p-value is 0.103 > 0.05, confirming this conclusion
- 95% lower bound: 3.910 (we are 95% confident the true mean is above 3.910 ml)

**Conclusion:** The data does not provide sufficient evidence that the mean caustic level exceeds 4 ml/litre.

### Step 4: Calculate the Power of the Test

Now ask: *Was our sample large enough to detect a meaningful difference?*

Suppose we define a difference of **0.35 ml/litre** as practically significant — i.e., we want to detect if the true mean is **μ_A = 4.35**.

**Find the critical sample mean (threshold for rejection):**

$$\bar{X}_{crit} = 4.0 + \frac{1.3}{\sqrt{30}} \times 1.645 = 4.0 + 0.39 = 4.39$$

So we only reject $H_0$ if the sample mean exceeds 4.39.

**Calculate β (probability of Type II error):**

$$\beta = P(\bar{X} < 4.39 \mid \mu = 4.35) = P\left(Z < \frac{4.39 - 4.35}{1.3/\sqrt{30}}\right) = P(Z < 0.17) = 0.5675$$

**Power:**

$$\text{Power} = 1 - 0.5675 = \mathbf{0.4325}$$

**Interpretation:** There is only a **43.25% chance** of correctly detecting a 0.35 ml difference with n = 30. This is quite low — the test is underpowered. We would ideally want power ≥ 0.80.

> **Annotation space:** *What would you recommend the inspector do? Collect a larger sample? Define a larger minimum effect size? Both options involve trade-offs.*

---

## Sample Size from Margin of Error

Power analysis is closely linked to **confidence intervals**. The margin of error (ME) in a confidence interval is:

$$ME = Z_{\alpha/2} \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Rearranging to find the required sample size for a desired ME:

$$n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \times \sigma}{ME}\right)^2$$

**Example:** An analyst wants to estimate mean voltage (σ = 22.5 volts) with a margin of error of **5 volts** at 95% confidence:

$$n = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 22.5}{5}\right)^2 = (8.82)^2 \approx 78 \text{ observations}$$

When $\sigma$ is **unknown**, replace $Z$ with the $t$-distribution critical value and $\sigma$ with the sample standard deviation $s$.

---

# Summary Points

- **Missing data** is common in real datasets — before removing or imputing, always visualise and understand the pattern.
- The **VIM** package functions (`aggr`, `barMiss`, `histMiss`, `marginplot`) help you explore *where* and *why* data is missing.
- **Complete case analysis** (`na.omit`) is simple but can bias results if the missing data is not random.
- **`irmi()`** performs iterative regression-based imputation; **MICE** performs multiple imputation and is more robust for uncertainty quantification.
- **Statistical power** (1 − β) is the probability of correctly detecting a real effect — aim for at least 0.80.
- Power depends on **four quantities**: sample size, effect size, α, and power (1 − β). Know three, find the fourth.
- Low power (like 43% in the example) means a test may fail to detect a real, meaningful difference — the sample was simply too small.
- You can use the **margin of error** formula to calculate the minimum sample size needed for a given precision, whether σ is known (Z-formula) or unknown (t-formula).

---

# Exercises to Try

1. Recalculate the power of the caustic material test if the minimum detectable increase is **0.1 ml** instead of 0.35 ml. *(Answer: 0.1112)*
2. An analyst wants to estimate mean voltage (σ = 22.5 volts). What sample size achieves a margin of error of **5 volts** at 95% confidence for a two-sided CI? *(Answer: n = 78)*
3. In the sleep dataset, which variable had the most missing values? Use `aggr()` to verify.
4. Why does the pooled MICE model have **larger standard errors** than the individual imputed models?

---
